stages.label: Starter
stages.description: Absolute zero — letters, sounds, first words. You will leave able to say hello, count, and name basic things. Mexican Spanish remains the default spoken target.

items.notes: Use '¿Cómo está usted?' formally.
Encantado if you're male, Encantada if female.
Staple of Mexican cuisine — say it right!
SER = permanent traits: nationality, identity
ESTAR = states, feelings, location
The tilde makes n sound like 'ny'
In Mexico, ll = y sound
Spanish j is a strong H
Practice rolling r slightly
H is always silent in Spanish

stages.label: Beginner
stages.description: A1–A2 level. Build your first real sentences, ask for things, handle a market or restaurant. Mexican Spanish remains the default spoken target.

items.notes: Al pastor = marinated pork on a vertical spit, very Mexican
Common street snack — grilled corn with mayo, cheese and chili
Literal: 'little cravings' — encompasses tacos, tlayudas, sopes etc.
Very common market expression in Mexico

items.meaning: Can you give me a discount?

items.notes: In Mexico 'camión' = bus

items.meaning: A room with a double bed
Can you bring up the luggage?

items.notes: Use 'gustan' (plural) for plural nouns

items.meaning: What do you do? / What are you doing?

items.target: Necesito algo para el dolor de cabeza
items.meaning: I need something for a headache

items.notes: Use usted with strangers, elders, authority

items.meaning: Please be so kind as to…

stages.label: Explorer
stages.description: A2–B1. Build connected sentences, discuss the past, navigate real social situations. Mexican Spanish remains the default spoken target.

items.notes: -ER verb: comer
IR/SER preterite: fui

items.meaning: I used to go to school
I'm going to travel to Mexico
What are you going to do?
I plan to stay one week

items.notes: Very popular Mexican beer drink

items.meaning: What do you have to snack on?
items.notes: 'Botanear' = to snack while drinking, very Mexican

items.target: La neta está muy buena la música
items.meaning: For real, the music is really good
items.notes: 'La neta' = the truth, really — Mexican slang
'Antro' = nightclub/bar in Mexico

items.target: Va la última y nos vamos

items.notes: Major cultural event in Mexico — like a debutante ball
Families get padrinos to sponsor parts of the party
Common phrase at family gatherings

items.target: ¡Ya están el mole y los tamales!
items.meaning: The mole and tamales are ready!

items.notes: 'Ojalá' comes from Arabic — very common in Spanish!

items.meaning: Hope it goes well for you
items.notes: Very common parting phrase
Mexico uses 'celular', not 'móvil'

items.meaning: I want to speak to the manager

items.target: Esto no es lo que pedí

items.meaning: What do you do for work?

stages.label: Speaker
stages.description: B1. Real conversations, mixed register unlocks here. Mexican slang, storytelling, complex situations.

items.notes: The most common Mexican informal address. Use only with friends.
Polite version of a stronger phrase
CRITICAL: 'ahorita' does NOT mean immediately. It's vague. Can be 5 min or 2 hours.
Extremely versatile Mexican exclamation
Very casual, use only with close friends
Can be affectionate between friends
'Hazme un paro' = do me a favor

items.target: Es raro que no haya llegado

items.meaning: I'm looking for someone who speaks English
Could you tell me where … is?

items.notes: 'A poco' = expression of mild surprise

items.target: Formal: Muchas gracias, que tenga buen día
items.meaning: Thank you, have a nice day
If I had money, I would travel

items.notes: In Mexico, 'autobús' is formal; 'camión' is everyday
Very common cheap transport in Mexico City
Safer than hailing street taxis in Mexico

items.meaning: How much will you charge me?
items.notes: Always negotiate taxi price upfront

items.meaning: I feel like / I want to…

items.target: Costar un ojo de la cara
items.meaning: To cost an arm and a leg

items.target: No hay mal que por bien no venga
items.meaning: Every cloud has a silver lining
To have your head in the clouds
To put your foot in your mouth

items.target: No tener pelos en la lengua
items.meaning: To not mince words / be blunt

items.notes: Basic health coverage in Mexico
Major Mexican cultural holiday — Nov 1-2
'Maestro' is a respectful way to address skilled workers
Can be slightly derogatory depending on context

stages.label: Scholar
stages.description: B2. Complex grammar, professional/academic Spanish, nuanced expression.

items.meaning: If I could, I would do it
I wish I had arrived earlier

items.target: Le envío este correo para informarle
items.meaning: I'm sending this email to inform you

items.notes: SER = inherent quality
ESTAR = current state
SER = clever by nature
ESTAR = ready at this moment

sessions.title: Mexico City vs North vs South

items.notes: Very uniquely Mexican — used instead of '¿Qué?'
Strong language — use with caution

items.meaning: to ask for vs to ask (question)
to know a fact vs to know a person
to leave (something) vs to leave (go out)
The place where I was born

stages.label: Specialist
stages.description: C1. Near-fluency. Nuanced natural speech, professional domains, culture deep dives.

items.meaning: To play dead / pretend not to hear
items.notes: Mexican expression

items.meaning: It should be pointed out that

items.notes: Complex cultural symbol — understand before using the term

items.meaning: The mixing of cultures / racial mixing
The thing is… / It's just that…

items.notes: Very common Mexican filler

items.target: Context read: boardroom → ¿Podría presentar los resultados?
Context read: friend → ¿Echamos un ojo a los números?
items.meaning: Wanna take a look at the numbers?

items.notes: Dropped syllables are normal in fast speech
Common in US-Mexico border communities

stages.label: Archivist
stages.description: C2. Native-level — literature, poetry, dialect mastery, sociolinguistics. The vault.

items.notes: 'Usted' evolved from 'vuestra merced'

items.meaning: In days of old / in the past
Full rhyme (same sound from last vowel)

items.target: El que con lobos anda, a aullar se enseña
items.meaning: You become like the company you keep

items.target: Camarón que se duerme, se lo lleva la corriente
items.meaning: The shrimp that falls asleep gets swept away — stay alert
items.notes: Classic Mexican proverb about being proactive

items.meaning: In bad times, put on a good face
Merging of ll/y sounds (most of Latin America)
Hey dude, how are you all doing?

items.notes: Extremely common in Mexican Spanish — softens requests

items.target: Full chain: Buenos días → ¿Cómo estás? → ¿Qué onda, güey?

items.meaning: Swear words / profanity (know what they mean, context matters)
items.notes: Understanding ≠ using. Knowing these helps you understand what people say around you.

items.meaning: It was Juan who did it

items.target: Ahorita, órale, chido, güey, qué onda, sale, no manches, híjole, va, la neta, chamba, cotorrear
items.meaning: Core 12 Mexican expressions — you know them all by now!

exchanges.prompt: Tell them you need a table for two.
exchanges.modelAnswer: Una mesa para dos, por favor.

exchanges.translation: Can I bring you something to drink?

exchanges.prompt: Order two waters and ask for the menu.
exchanges.modelAnswer: Dos aguas, por favor, y la carta.
Tres tacos de al pastor, por favor.

exchanges.prompt: Say yes with everything, but ask if the salsa is spicy.
exchanges.modelAnswer: Sí, con todo. ¿Está muy picosa la salsa?

exchanges.notes: Güero/güera = fair-skinned person, used as a friendly address for foreigners

exchanges.prompt: Ask for a kilo of tomatoes and half a kilo of avocados.
exchanges.modelAnswer: Un kilo de tomates y medio kilo de aguacates.

exchanges.prompt: Ask if they can give you a discount.
Stop someone politely and ask where the nearest metro station is.
exchanges.modelAnswer: Disculpe, ¿sabe dónde está el metro más cercano?

exchanges.text: Sí, está a tres cuadras, doble a la izquierda en el semáforo.
exchanges.translation: Yes, it's three blocks away, turn left at the traffic light.

exchanges.prompt: Thank them and confirm: 'three blocks and left at the light?'
exchanges.modelAnswer: Muchas gracias. ¿Tres cuadras y a la izquierda en el semáforo?

exchanges.translation: Good afternoon, welcome. Do you have a reservation?

exchanges.prompt: Yes, a reservation under your name for two nights.
exchanges.modelAnswer: Sí, tengo una reservación a mi nombre, dos noches.

exchanges.text: Perfecto. ¿Necesita que le suba el equipaje?
exchanges.translation: Perfect. Do you need someone to bring up your luggage?

exchanges.prompt: Say no thank you, and ask for the wifi password.
exchanges.modelAnswer: No, gracias. ¿Me puede dar la contraseña del wifi?

exchanges.translation: Hello, how can I help you?

exchanges.prompt: Say you have a headache and ask if they have ibuprofen.

exchanges.translation: Yes, we have generic 400mg. Do you need a prescription?

exchanges.prompt: Ask if this one needs a prescription.
exchanges.modelAnswer: ¿Este necesita receta o es sin receta?

exchanges.prompt: Order a mezcal for yourself and ask what they have to snack on.
exchanges.modelAnswer: Un mezcal para mí, y ¿qué tienen para botanear?

exchanges.text: Tenemos chicharrones, cacahuates y tacos de canasta.
exchanges.translation: We have pork rinds, peanuts, and basket tacos.

exchanges.prompt: Say it all sounds good. Order the pork rinds and toast to the night.
exchanges.modelAnswer: Todo suena bien, los chicharrones por favor. ¡Salud!

exchanges.prompt: Say you need help, you were robbed.

exchanges.translation: Do you want me to call the police?

exchanges.prompt: Yes, please call the police and ask where the nearest hospital is.
exchanges.modelAnswer: Sí, por favor llame a la policía. ¿Dónde está el hospital más cercano?

exchanges.prompt: Say the address and ask if traffic is heavy.
exchanges.modelAnswer: Vamos a la calle Reforma 120. ¿Hay mucho tráfico?

phraseLibrary.grammarNote: Costar is an o→ue stem-changing verb.
'Ahorita' is vague — doesn't mean immediately in Mexico.

phraseLibrary.translation: Can you bring me the check?
phraseLibrary.grammarNote: Formal indirect object: 'me puede dar' = can you give me.
Órale is a uniquely Mexican exclamation/agreement.
Command form: 'hable' is the formal imperative of hablar.

phraseLibrary.phrase: ¿Me da dos tacos al pastor, por favor?
phraseLibrary.translation: Can I get two tacos al pastor, please?
I will be right back / I will come back in a bit
phraseLibrary.grammarNote: Ahorita in Mexican Spanish depends on context; here it means soon.

phraseLibrary.translation: Where does the bus stop / pass by?
phraseLibrary.grammarNote: Camión commonly means bus in Mexico.
Useful for taxis, kiosks, and small stores.
